findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive grep AC for each reconcile comment replacement asserting the new text exists (e.g., `grep -q 'reconcile_helpers.py' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py && grep -q 'reconcile_helpers.py' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/run_differs.py && grep -q 'reconcile_helpers.py' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound.py`).
evidence: AC: `! grep -q 'route_inbound_probe stays here' reconcile.py && ! grep -q 'reconcile.py keeps' run_differs.py && ! grep -q 'probe execution lives in reconcile.route_inbound_probe' apply_inbound.py`
What section step 5: 'update the THREE stale comments so they say route_inbound_probe now lives in reconcile_helpers.py and is re-exported through reconcile.py'
ABSENCE: no positive grep asserting 'reconcile_helpers.py' appears in each of the three files after the edit
findings.finding: The ACs for the three stale-comment replacements use only negative grep (old text gone) but do NOT verify the replacement text was correctly inserted. An executor could delete the comment lines entirely and satisfy all three ACs without ever writing the intended 'reconcile_helpers.py / re-exported through reconcile.py' replacement text.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — fifth bullet (`! grep -q 'route_inbound_probe stays here' ...`)
scenarios: Agent deletes the comment lines entirely; all three negative greps pass; reconcile residency is now undocumented rather than correctly documented.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the fifth AC bullet: `&& grep -q 're-exported through reconcile' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py && grep -q 'reconcile_helpers' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/run_differs.py && grep -q 'reconcile_helpers' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add positive grep ACs confirming the replacement prose was actually written, e.g., `grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' docs/architecture.md && grep -q '800' docs/architecture.md` and `grep -q 'admin' AGENTS.md && grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' AGENTS.md`.
evidence: What step 3: 'update the Module-size policy prose to describe the ABSOLUTE 800 cap (no allowlist), the single-source .github/module-size-limit.txt, and that changing the limit requires an admin to override the CI gate'
What step 4: 'state the cap is absolute and the limit is admin-locked'
ABSENCE: no positive grep asserting 'module-size-limit.txt' or '800' or 'admin' appears in docs/architecture.md or AGENTS.md after the edit
findings.finding: The docs/architecture.md and AGENTS.md ACs verify only that old text is absent (no 'Current offenders' section, no 'module-size-allowlist' reference) but do NOT verify that the required new positive content was actually written — specifically: the 800 LOC absolute cap, the single-source `.github/module-size-limit.txt` reference, and the admin-lock description for changing the limit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — third and fourth bullets (`! grep -q 'Current offenders' docs/architecture.md && ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' docs/architecture.md`; `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' AGENTS.md`)
scenarios: Executor deletes the sections entirely rather than rewriting them; both negative-grep ACs pass; the architecture doc no longer documents the module-size policy at all.
Executor writes incomplete replacement prose omitting the admin-lock description; ACs still pass.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the third AC bullet: `&& grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' docs/architecture.md && grep -qE 'admin.*override|override.*admin' docs/architecture.md`; add to the fourth AC bullet: `&& grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' AGENTS.md && grep -q 'admin' AGENTS.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC3 to add a positive grep verifying replacement prose exists (e.g., `grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' docs/architecture.md`) and extend AC4 to verify the absolute-cap sentence was added to AGENTS.md (e.g., `grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' AGENTS.md`).
evidence: AC3 verify: `! grep -q 'Current offenders' docs/architecture.md && ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' docs/architecture.md`
AC4 verify: `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' AGENTS.md`

What3: 'update the \'## Module-size policy\' prose to describe the ABSOLUTE 800 cap (no allowlist), the single-source `.github/module-size-limit.txt`, and that changing the limit requires an admin to override the CI gate'
What4: 'remove the `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` reference; state the cap is absolute and the limit is admin-locked'
E1 checklist: 'For migrations, criteria verify BOTH removal and replacement'
findings.finding: AC3 and AC4 verify only removal of allowlist references (negative greps) but not that the described replacement content was actually added — What3 says to update the prose to describe 'the ABSOLUTE 800 cap (no allowlist), the single-source .github/module-size-limit.txt, and that changing the limit requires an admin to override the CI gate'; What4 says to 'state the cap is absolute and the limit is admin-locked'. Neither AC verifies the presence of this replacement prose.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria items 3 and 4 vs What items 3 and 4
scenarios: An implementer deletes the allowlist reference from AGENTS.md but forgets to add the replacement sentence about the absolute cap; AC4's negative grep passes, but the doc is now incomplete with no covering verification.
docs/architecture.md's Module-size policy section is left with vague or absent replacement prose; AC3 passes its grep but the doc is misleading.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3 verify: `&& grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' docs/architecture.md && grep -q 'admin' docs/architecture.md`. Add to AC4 verify: `&& grep -q 'module-size-limit.txt' AGENTS.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive-assertion AC for each of the three comment rewrites — e.g. `grep -q 'reconcile_helpers' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py` — so that the ACs confirm replacement text was actually written, not just that old text was removed.
evidence: Plan says 'update the THREE stale comments so they say route_inbound_probe now lives in `reconcile_helpers.py` and is re-exported through reconcile.py' — replacement wording is paraphrased but never quoted or normalized.

AC #5: '! grep -q "route_inbound_probe stays here" src/.../reconcile.py && ! grep -q "reconcile.py keeps" src/.../run_differs.py && ! grep -q "probe execution lives in reconcile.route_inbound_probe" src/.../apply_inbound.py' — all three checks are purely negative; no grep asserts presence of replacement text.

apply_inbound.py line 279 confirmed: 'probe execution lives in reconcile.route_inbound_probe' — this is the docstring for _apply_inbound_probe; simply removing it leaves the function undocumented with no explanation of where probe execution actually occurs.
ABSENCE: No AC of the form 'grep -q "reconcile_helpers" src/.../apply_inbound.py' to confirm the corrected routing reference was written.
findings.finding: The plan specifies WHAT old comment text to remove from all three reconciler files but never specifies WHAT replacement text to write. The ACs verify only deletion (negative greps) — there is no positive assertion that any replacement comment was actually written. An executor following the ACs mechanically could delete all three comments in their entirety (leaving no replacement) and pass every AC, satisfying the letter of the plan while violating its intent.
findings.location: ## What, item 5 (the three stale comments); ## Acceptance Criteria, AC #5
scenarios: Executor deletes all three comments outright (blank lines or no comment) — all ACs pass, but the functions lose their architectural context comments entirely.
Executor rewrites the comments incorrectly (e.g. says 'reconcile_helpers' but drops the re-export caveat) — ACs still pass, but the replacement is misleading to future readers.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add three companion positive-grep ACs alongside the existing negative ones, for example:
```
&& grep -q 'reconcile_helpers.py' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py
&& grep -q 'reconcile_helpers' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/run_differs.py
&& grep -q 'reconcile_helpers' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound.py
```
Also, pin the exact replacement wording for each comment in the plan body (even one sentence per file) so executors don't have to infer the correct form.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the dual-ownership conflict: either (a) remove the reconcile.py and run_differs.py comment fixes from this leaf and let 3c7f own them exclusively (this leaf then only adds the apply_inbound.py third fix if it is re-parented appropriately), or (b) transfer full ownership of all three comment fixes to this leaf and remove them from 3c7f's What and ACs (requiring a 3c7f plan update).
evidence: Parent 3c7f AC: 'The two stale route_inbound_probe residency comments are replaced in BOTH files. Verified by: grep -c route_inbound_probe stays here src/.../reconcile.py returns 0 AND grep -c reconcile.py keeps src/.../run_differs.py returns 0'

Leaf AC: 'ALL THREE stale reconcile residency comments are replaced (verify: ! grep -q route_inbound_probe stays here .../reconcile.py && ! grep -q reconcile.py keeps .../run_differs.py && ! grep -q probe execution lives in reconcile.route_inbound_probe .../apply_inbound.py)'
Both the parent and the leaf assert the same post-condition on reconcile.py and run_differs.py
findings.finding: The leaf claims to deliver the same two route_inbound_probe residency comment fixes (reconcile.py, run_differs.py) that parent 3c7f's own Acceptance Criteria also require. This creates a direct AC contradiction: 3c7f AC5 verifies 'grep -c route_inbound_probe stays here reconcile.py returns 0 AND grep -c reconcile.py keeps run_differs.py returns 0', but if this leaf lands those fixes first, 3c7f's AC5 is pre-satisfied by a sibling — the parent and leaf both claim ownership of the same deliverable.
findings.location: Leaf 'What' item 5 (reconcile.py, run_differs.py); parent 3c7f AC5 and 'Stale-comment updates in scope'
scenarios: If this leaf lands before 3c7f, 3c7f's comment-fix AC passes trivially (the files were already changed by this leaf), making 3c7f's contribution to those ACs invisible and its verification meaningless.
If 3c7f lands first and makes the fixes, this leaf's AC on the same grep passes trivially without the leaf doing any work — the AC becomes vacuous.
Reviewers and CI cannot tell which ticket 'owns' the comment fix; revert of either ticket produces inconsistent states.

evidence: verify command `! grep -q 'Current offenders' docs/architecture.md && ! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' docs/architecture.md` greps the unanchored word 'Current' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_Current'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"Current":')

verify command `! grep -q 'route_inbound_probe stays here' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/reconcile.py && ! grep -q 'reconcile.py keeps' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/run_differs.py && ! grep -q 'probe execution lives in reconcile.route_inbound_probe' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound.py` greps the unanchored word 'route_inbound_probe' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_route_inbound_probe'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"route_inbound_probe":')
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a repo-wide grep AC (e.g., `! git grep -rq 'module-size-allowlist\|module-size-ceilings' -- ':!*.git'`) to catch any remaining references to the deleted files outside docs/architecture.md and AGENTS.md.
evidence: AC greps: `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' docs/architecture.md` and `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' AGENTS.md` — these are file-scoped, not repo-wide
ABSENCE: no `git grep` or repo-wide search for 'module-size-allowlist' or 'module-size-ceilings' across all files is specified
The plan notes Phase 2a's gate already stopped reading the files but does not audit whether other docs/YAMLs still name them
findings.finding: The ACs only grep docs/architecture.md and AGENTS.md for stale references to the deleted files; other locations (CI workflow YAMLs, README, CHANGELOG, inline source comments outside the three named reconciler files, or any other docs) are not checked, so deleted-artifact references could be stranded elsewhere in the repo.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — verification greps for allowlist references
scenarios: A contributor reads a CI workflow YAML or README that still mentions module-size-allowlist.txt as the escape hatch and follows stale instructions
A future grep-based search for the deleted filenames finds unexpected hits, causing confusion about whether the files were truly retired
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: `! git grep -rql 'module-size-allowlist\.txt\|module-size-ceilings\.txt'` (exit 1 means references found). Also run this manually before merging to identify any CI YAML, README, or CHANGELOG hits that need a follow-up edit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the plan title (or add a subtitle) to reflect the reconciler comment fixes, e.g. 'Phase 2b: delete orphaned allowlist/ceilings files, prune module-size docs, and fix stale reconcile residency comments (3c7f)'.
evidence: Title: 'Phase 2b: delete the drained allowlist/ceilings files + prune module-size docs'
What item 5: 'Incidental (unblocks 3c7f): update the THREE stale comments so they say route_inbound_probe now lives in reconcile_helpers.py...'
The title names only file deletion and doc pruning; no mention of source-code comment changes.
findings.finding: The plan title ('delete the drained allowlist/ceilings files + prune module-size docs') does not cover What item 5 — fixing three stale reconciler source comments (reconcile.py, run_differs.py, apply_inbound.py). Reviewers or routing logic relying on the title will not expect source-code edits; the change does MORE than the title promises.
findings.location: What — item 5 (Incidental) and the plan title
scenarios: A reviewer approving this change based on the title alone may not scrutinize the reconciler source edits, potentially missing an incorrect comment update that silently misrepresents the module's API surface.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the plan title to: 'Phase 2b: delete orphaned allowlist/ceilings files + prune module-size docs + fix stale reconcile residency comments (3c7f)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `pyproject.toml` to the edit set (or explicitly acknowledge it as out-of-scope) — the comment at line 292 references `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` and will be a dangling reference after the git rm.

Plan What section lists: AGENTS.md, docs/architecture.md, .github files, test file, three reconciler source files — pyproject.toml is absent.
AC4 greps only AGENTS.md: `! grep -q 'module-size-allowlist' AGENTS.md` — pyproject.toml is not covered.
findings.finding: pyproject.toml contains a comment at line 292 referencing '.github/module-size-allowlist.txt' (describing a historical analogy between the ruff strangler allowlist and the module-size allowlist) that will become a dangling reference after the git rm. The plan's edit set and ACs do not enumerate pyproject.toml as a consumer requiring update.
scenarios: After deletion, pyproject.toml:292 contains a reference to a non-existent file, confusing future contributors or tooling that audits dangling file references.
A future grep for 'module-size-allowlist' across the repo will surface pyproject.toml as a seemingly stale hit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `apply_inbound.py` line 187's inline comment (`route_inbound_probe (reconcile.py) emits`) to What item 5 as a fourth stale comment to fix, and extend the AC5 grep to also assert `! grep -q 'route_inbound_probe (reconcile.py)' src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound.py`.
evidence: Plan What item 5 says 'update the THREE stale comments' and lists apply_inbound.py '~line 279, the `probe execution lives in reconcile.route_inbound_probe` docstring'
apply_inbound.py:187: '# Canonical key is ``reason`` — route_inbound_probe (reconcile.py) emits' — says reconcile.py, but function lives in reconcile_helpers.py (re-exported)

reconcile.py:67 confirms: `route_inbound_probe = _helpers.route_inbound_probe` (sourced from reconcile_helpers.py)

AC5's grep is scoped only to the line-279 text and will not catch or verify the line-187 fix, so an executor following the plan exactly will leave a stale comment behind.

findings.finding: The plan does not address a stale comment in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound.py` line 187, which says `route_inbound_probe (reconcile.py) emits` — attributing the function to `reconcile.py` when the plan asserts it has moved to `reconcile_helpers.py`. This is the same category of stale residency comment the plan explicitly fixes in two other files but misses here.
findings.location: What §5 (incidental comment fixes); plan asserts 'route_inbound_probe now lives in reconcile_helpers.py'
findings.suggested_fix: In What §5, add a third bullet: `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound.py` (the `route_inbound_probe (reconcile.py) emits` comment at line 187).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out note to the plan (e.g., 'Rollback: `git revert <this commit>` restores both files; the Phase 2a gate ignores them so reverting this commit has no gate impact') so the destructive step has a stated reversibility mechanism.
evidence: What item 1: '`git rm` `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` and `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt`'
Notes: 'Mechanical deletion + doc/comment edits; no runtime code logic changes'
Notes: 'Phase 2a's new gate does not read the deleted files, so their removal is green'
ABSENCE: no rollback/revert instruction anywhere in the plan
T4 checklist: 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract — acknowledgement alone is insufficient'
findings.finding: The plan performs an irreversible destructive step (git rm of two files) but provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan. The plan justifies the deletion and states no consumers exist, but T4 requires an explicit rollback mechanism, not just acknowledgement of justified destruction.
scenarios: A previously unknown CI script or external tool is found to reference the deleted files after the commit lands; without a stated back-out path, the team has no documented procedure to recover quickly.
The assumption that Phase 2a fully orphaned both files proves incorrect for one of them (e.g., a branch not yet merged still reads ceilings.txt); a rollback note would make recovery unambiguous.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' bullet to the Notes section: 'Back-out: `git revert <commit-sha>` restores both files intact; since Phase 2a's gate does not read them, reverting this commit is safe and side-effect-free.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Re-parent this leaf directly under the grandparent epic (716f) as a standalone Phase 2b story, OR restructure so that only deliverables genuinely within 3c7f's scope (the two comment fixes) remain under 3c7f, with items 1–4 broken into a separate Phase 2 child of the epic.
evidence: Parent 3c7f What: 'graduate reconcile.py off the module-size gate by extracting a cohesive cluster of 10 pure/IO leaf UTILITY symbols into a NEW sibling leaf module reconcile_helpers.py' — no mention of deleting the allowlist/ceilings files or updating AGENTS.md
Parent 3c7f AC: 'reconcile.py removed from the module-size gate files and its remedy row deleted from docs' — removal of a row, not deletion of the file
Parent 3c7f AC: 'reconcile_helpers.py is NOT added to either gate file' — both files are expected to still exist after 3c7f lands

Grandparent 716f Phase 2: 'delete the allowlist+ceilings mechanism, replace the contract test ... prune the architecture.md offender table, and add the limit-lock gate' — these are explicitly Phase 2 items, not within 3c7f (Phase 1)
Leaf What item 1: 'git rm .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt'
Leaf What item 2: 'tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py: add a test asserting the allowlist + ceilings files no longer exist'
Leaf What item 3: 'docs/architecture.md: delete the ... section ... update the ... prose to describe the ABSOLUTE 800 cap'
Leaf What item 4: 'AGENTS.md: update the module-size policy paragraph — remove the .github/module-size-allowlist.txt reference'
findings.finding: The leaf's four primary deliverables (deleting .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt, adding an absence test, rewriting docs/architecture.md module-size policy prose, and updating AGENTS.md) are entirely outside parent 3c7f's declared scope. Parent 3c7f is a Phase 1 module-split story whose scope is creating reconcile_helpers.py, shrinking reconcile.py, and removing the reconcile.py row from the gate files — not deleting those files or updating AGENTS.md. These are Phase 2/epic-level deliverables that belong directly under the grandparent epic (716f), not under 3c7f.
scenarios: If this leaf is reviewed/landed as a child of 3c7f, its scope diverges from 3c7f's contract, making 3c7f's plan-review attestation stale and creating sequencing ambiguity about when Phase 2 work lands relative to Phase 1 work.

Parent 3c7f's own ACs assume the allowlist and ceilings files still exist after its change (they verify row counts within those files) — deleting the files in the same child story contradicts those ACs.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the leaf's parent_id from 3c7f to 716f. This leaf is a Phase 2 story and its natural parent is the epic (716f), whose Phase 2 decomposition explicitly calls for exactly these deletions and doc updates. The two comment-fix items (which overlap with 3c7f's own ACs) should either remain in 3c7f and be dropped from this leaf, or be transferred here with 3c7f's corresponding ACs updated to reflect that this leaf now owns them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add apply_inbound.py to parent 3c7f's scope (update 3c7f's What, file_impact, and ACs, then re-review 3c7f before re-reviewing this leaf), or handle the apply_inbound.py fix in a separate minimal leaf under the epic.

Leaf What item 5: 'apply_inbound.py (~line 279, the probe execution lives in reconcile.route_inbound_probe docstring)' — explicitly a third file not in the parent
Leaf notes: 'the third was found in review' — acknowledges this is additive, outside 3c7f's original scope
findings.finding: The leaf adds a third stale-comment fix in apply_inbound.py (~line 279) that is not present in parent 3c7f's What or Acceptance Criteria. Parent 3c7f's 'Stale-comment updates in scope' section lists exactly TWO files (reconcile.py and run_differs.py); apply_inbound.py is not mentioned anywhere in 3c7f.
findings.location: Leaf 'What' item 5 (apply_inbound.py ~line 279); parent 3c7f 'Stale-comment updates in scope'
scenarios: If apply_inbound.py is edited under this leaf but the parent (3c7f) has no awareness of or AC for it, the parent's plan-review attestation does not cover this change, and the file_impact manifest is inconsistent with what actually lands.

findings.finding: apply_inbound.py:279 contains a stale comment 'probe execution lives in reconcile.route_inbound_probe' which incorrectly names reconcile.py as the home of route_inbound_probe, but after the 3c7f split the function lives in reconcile_helpers.py (re-exported in reconcile.py). The plan enumerates only reconcile.py:177 and run_differs.py:16 as stale residency comments, missing this third instance in apply_inbound.py.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
